title: Tech boss backs Aussie AI amid tax fears
full_text:
Amazon Web Services' new Australian boss is confident local companies can become artificial intelligence leaders, not just consumers, despite fears Jim Chalmers’s tax overhaul will turn the country into an innovation backwater.
Chris Casey this week succeeded Rianne van Veldhuizen as AWS’s managing director for Australia and New Zealand as the tech behemoth plans to spend $20bn on building local AI infrastructure.
Mr Casey said the spend was justified, as Australian companies show no sign of pulling back on investing in AI despite the Treasurer scrapping the discount on capital gains tax.
“The companies that we work with are not even sure what the total addressable market is for them with regard to AI,” Mr Casey told this masthead on the eve of the AWS Summit in Sydney.
“Whether they’re a software company, a retailer or a bank, they are reimagining their end-consumer experience. That’s not necessarily about a specific piece of regulation that might be slowing down those conversations at a board level.”
Dr Chalmers is betting on a pipeline of data centre projects and renewable energy developments to carry business investment.
Companies including Oliver Curtis’s Firmus Technologies, the Blackstone-owned AirTrunk and Craig Scroggie’s listed NextDC locked in an arms race to establish Australia as a regional headquarters for the data centre industry.
Anthony Albanese joined AWS chief executive Matt Garman in Seattle earlier this year to announce the $20bn investment in Australian data centre infrastructure across Melbourne and Sydney over the next five years. This is on top of the $18.9bn it has spent on cloud computing in Australia since 2012.
But tech entrepreneurs and investors have roundly criticised Dr Chalmers’s move to replace the capital gains tax with indexation, given founders often start businesses with little upfront capital and it will skewer the high-risk, high-reward model of investing.
Goldman Sachs cautioned this would move Australia “from its current competitive position on capital gains to possibly becoming the nation with the highest taxation on gains globally”.
A last-minute bid from some founders, including Thomas Kelly from Heidi Health, has prompted the government to consider exempting early-stage ventures and start-ups from the changes.
“Given the unique characteristics of the tech and start-up sector, the government will consult on the interaction of the capital gains tax reforms and incentives for investment in early-stage and start-up businesses,” the budget documents said.
But Mr Casey, who spent two years leading AWS’s partner business across Asia Pacific and Japan, working closely with Canva, Atlassian and other companies, said the real bottleneck was not tax policy. It was skills.
AWS has commissioned research showing that almost half of Australia’s population say personal AI adoption is running ahead of enterprise adoption.
The skills gap is the real brake on translating AI’s promise to inject up to $600bn a year into the economy by the end of the decade.
Tesla chair Robyn Denholm said government efforts to solve the housing crisis, including overhauling capital gains tax, are misguided if they are not underpinned by high-value jobs.
“(Without) meaningful jobs, it doesn’t matter if housing is affordable because they won’t be able to afford it anyway. So the horse has got to be before the cart,” said Ms Denholm, who oversaw the government’s Ambitious Australia report that examined the nation’s research and development system.
Mr Casey said the skills gap was “one of the main reasons we’re continuing to invest in our skills and training programs — not only for corporations, but for individuals”.
He said AWS’s ‘AI Spring’ initiative is targeting schools, parents and teachers on responsible AI programming, and AWS has already trained 400,000 Australians.
It has also trained 100,000 New Zealanders – two years ahead of a commitment made under a joint agreement with the New Zealand government.
“All of the great innovations have come with incremental progress that then builds up into something meaningful,” Mr Casey said.
“The companies really delivering on the promise are finding a business objective – and then using AI to enable it."
Mr Casey also unveiled nine new renewable energy projects to add a gigawatt of energy capacity – enough to power about 500,000 Australian homes annually – as AWS attempts to keep pace with the AI-driven surge in power demand without opening the nuclear debate that was decisively closed at the federal election.
It is also investing in less power-hungry hardware at its data centres.
AWS’s Trainium 3 chip s, Mr Casey said, are four times more energy-efficient than previous generations. And he said the gap between energy demand and hardware efficiency would continue to narrow.
“We’re continuing to take a multipronged approach within our data centres to make sure they’re the most energy efficient as they possibly can be. So continuing to build efficient infrastructure, of course, continuing to invest in our chip technology … and of course, continuing to bring new carbon-free energy online.”
Mr Casey said 80 per cent of Australian businesses were experiencing “measurable productivity gains from their AI investments”.
AWS research found that most Australians believed AI could help societal challenges, including preventing or curing disease (63 per cent), improving access to healthcare (62 per cent) and driving economic growth (61 per cent).
“This isn’t a country that sees AI as a threat. This is a country that sees AI as the answer. The people closest to AI aren’t fearing it; they’re thriving because of it,” he said.
Nationwide News Pty Ltd.

summary: Amazon Web Services' new Australian managing director, Chris Casey, expresses confidence in local companies becoming AI leaders despite concerns over tax reforms, while highlighting the need for skills development and announcing a $20bn investment in AI infrastructure.
author: Jared Lynch
heading: Data Science and AI
key_points: AWS plans to invest $20bn in Australian AI infrastructure over the next five years.
Concerns arise from Treasurer Jim Chalmers' capital gains tax reforms, which may hinder investment in startups.
AWS has trained 400,000 Australians in AI skills and is launching new renewable energy projects to meet rising power demands.
